item.title: How Trillionaires Are Really Made
item.description: Some may argue that the world’s first trillionaire is a story of technological triumph and a win for American capitalism. But markets have not bet on cutting-edge technology or a company too big to fail, but rather on the idea of a mega-monopolist with wealth and influence so vast that the US government will not allow him to fail.

item.title: Cities Show How Climate Action Makes Life Better
item.description: Cities have shown that a combination of adaptation measures and energy efficiency can save lives, improve well-being, reduce energy costs, and create conditions for sustainable growth. A new partnership seeks to help mayors and city leaders scale up these efforts through technical and financial assistance.

item.title: The Myth of Global Chaos
item.description: Today’s seemingly nonstop international tumult is not as unintelligible as it seems. What pundits lazily describe as chaos is the culmination of developments that were long in the making and arrived at a time when the international system was no longer able to prevent or mitigate geopolitical shocks.

item.title: The Microfinance Debate Is Missing the Point
item.description: For too long, policymakers, economists, and investors have focused on whether microfinance actually helps people, reducing a complex issue to a meaningless yes-or-no verdict. The most important questions concern how loans are designed, delivered, and regulated, who receives them, and what they are used for.

item.title: Turning Latin America’s Resilience Into Growth
item.description: Economic stabilization has been a significant achievement for Latin America and the Caribbean, a region with a long history of financial crises. But these countries need a well-sequenced reform agenda to attract private investment and create the growth engine that will translate hard-won gains into higher living standards.

item.title: The World Cup vs. the Extremists
item.description: Germany’s 2026 World Cup team, like so many others in the tournament, shows that loving one’s country and welcoming newcomers are not incompatible impulses. Although activists on the far left and the far right would have us believe otherwise, most ordinary people implicitly recognize this.

item.title: Beyond Brexit—and Back to Europe
item.description: Those who follow only intergovernmental negotiations or debates in the media might think that nothing has changed in the decade since the United Kingdom's voters decided to leave the European Union. Yet new polling finds that large majorities on both sides of the English Channel now support re-engagement.

item.title: Trump’s March of Folly in Iran
item.description: In promoting his “deal” with Iran, US President Donald Trump has tried to dress up a humiliating defeat as a victory. While it’s tempting to blame this outcome on incompetent leadership, it stems more from the policies and institutions that have allowed entertainers and profiteers to rise to power.

item.title: Alan Greenspan’s Mixed Legacy
item.description: The longtime Federal Reserve chair inferred from past crises that lightly regulated markets, while prone to excesses, could right themselves sufficiently to avoid imperiling the financial system and the economy. The correct lesson would have been that markets require strict regulation, and that competent technocrats are essential.

item.title: Will AI Yield Abundance Without Purpose?
item.description: Work and employment play a uniquely powerful role in structuring people’s days and conferring a sense of belonging, status, and self-worth. Although AI boosters insist that people would organically find new sources of meaning if most jobs are automated, there is no reason to assume so.

item.title: To Avoid Climate Peril, the World Must Electrify
item.description: The war in the Gulf has unleashed powerful market forces that the rest of the world can harness to accelerate electrification and keep the Paris climate agreement's primary objectives on track. Doing so will not only mitigate climate change, but also strengthen economic resilience worldwide.

item.title: What the Iran War Taught the World About Food Security
item.description: Perhaps the most unsettling lesson from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is the gap between what we knew and how we prepared. Countries should use whatever easing of immediate pressures occurs to strengthen resilience before the next shock arrives.

item.title: The Orange Bandit
item.description: According to the Roman Emperor Tiberius, a ruler can either “shear the sheep” for many years, by encouraging productive activity from which to generate tax revenues, or he can “skin them alive” just once. By brazenly using the US government to pillage the country, President Donald Trump has apparently chosen the knife.

item.title: How (Not) to Conserve Tropical Forests
item.description: Although the Tropical Forest Forever Facility that emerged from the last United Nations Climate Conference is unlikely to succeed, critically important global conservation efforts are not doomed. Through sustainability-linked sovereign bonds and loans, tropical countries can overcome the TFFF’s fatal flaws.

item.title: Was Brexit Inevitable?
item.description: If counterfactual history is motivated by a refusal to accept what many have deemed inevitable, it is newly relevant now that the West is marking the tenth anniversary of the Brexit referendum. Even if Britain's fateful choice was caused by deeper structural and historical forces, that does not justify fatalism.

item.title: Questioning the Just War Doctrine
item.description: With war becoming more frequent and more lethal to civilians, Pope Leo XIV and the College of Cardinals are wading into the debate over its legitimacy. The best outcome of the “consistory” Leo is convening in in late June would be to authorize an overhaul of the just war doctrine for the modern era—and ask the necessary questions.

item.title: Bosnia Must Stand on Its Own
item.description: The Trump administration's recent, apparently corrupt intervention in Bosnia and Herzegovina could have been an opportunity finally to put the country on a path to full independence after more than 30 years of being a ward of the international community. Instead, the vicious cycle of dependence looks set to continue.

item.title: The Strategic Logic of the AI Arms Race
item.description: AI and drones are rapidly transforming warfare, shifting military power toward countries that can combine manufacturing scale, advanced AI systems, and battlefield data. The United States and Europe, hobbled by political dysfunction and obsolete military structures, remain dangerously unprepared.

item.title: The Meaning of Limited Government—Then and Now
item.description: In 1776, Adam Smith and America’s founders envisioned an economy that allowed individuals to pursue their own material well-being, with minimal government intervention. Today, America’s Republican Party claims to be committed to restoring that laissez-faire ideal, even as they make a mockery of its animating principles.

item.title: AI Sovereignty Is About Options, Not Ownership
item.description: The US government’s abrupt decision to suspend foreign access to Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models clarifies what “AI sovereignty” is really about. In the emerging AI economy, competitive advantage will come not from owning a single model, but from being able to evaluate, select, and orchestrate many models.
